interactions.summary: Jiang continues reading the Amazonian creation myth in which Romi Kumu destroys and remakes the world, then begins questioning students about what creation myths do.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang reading Wade Davis and then questioning the class.

interactions.summary: Through classroom prompts and Jiang's answers, the creation myth becomes a model of legal order and religious authority: it explains rules, taboos, grandness, completeness, and unity.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang leads; student responses are mostly inaudible and folded into his restatement.

interactions.summary: Jiang reads and interprets Wade Davis's description of a world saturated with meaning, where visible things point to a more-real shadow dimension available to the shaman.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang reading quoted material with interpretive gloss.

interactions.summary: Jiang asks what is surprising about the religion and the shamans' appearance, then argues that the religion is imaginative, complex, carefully organized, and status-bearing rather than primitive.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang with brief partial student answers.

speaker_notes.note: The lecture includes classroom questions and short student answers, but the student speech is not reliably captured; Jiang restates the answers he wants to use.
Several passages are read from Wade Davis and should not be attributed as Jiang's own coined language without noting the quoted source.

claims.claim: Jiang argues that creation myths explain not only how the world formed and where people come from, but also the legal structure and taboos needed for society to function.
He says a powerful religion must feel as correct as science by achieving grandness, completeness, and unity.
Grandness means a vast cosmology; completeness means everything visible must be explained; unity means a beginning and an end that hold the whole system together.
The Amazonian religious world is presented as one in which every rock, waterfall, plant, and animal is saturated with meaning and belongs to one connected whole.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's interpretation of Wade Davis in this lecture.

claims.claim: Jiang says the visible world is only one level of perception and that a more-real shadow dimension, where the soul resides, is visible to the shaman.
He rejects the prejudice that non-technological peoples are stupid, arguing that their religious imagination is sophisticated, complex, and vivid.
claims.temporal_scope: Normative interpretive claim in this lecture.

claims.claim: The shamans' clothing, posture, symmetry, and status markers are treated as evidence of an organized ritual order.
claims.temporal_scope: Interpretive claim from classroom image analysis.

signature_moments.moment: Creation myth becomes law: the story explains why incest, child-killing, and disorder belong to evil.
signature_moments.source_phrase: explaining to you the legal system, the rules, and why we have them
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It shows myth functioning as social architecture rather than mere origin story.

signature_moments.moment: A religion must become as correct as science in the believer's mind.
signature_moments.source_phrase: your religion is correct, okay? It is as correct as science
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This gives Jiang a test for religious authority that later explains why ritual nonbelief is dangerous.

signature_moments.moment: Behind every tangible thing is a shadow dimension that holds its real essence.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Behind every tangible form... is a shadow dimension
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This image is one of the lecture's clearest formulations of religious imagination.

signature_moments.moment: The quoted cosmology turns tobacco powder into the skull and brain of the sun.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the skull and brain of the sun
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The strange concrete image preserves the vividness Jiang is trying to make students recognize.

signature_moments.moment: No cars or cell phones does not mean no intelligence; the religion itself is evidence of sophistication.
signature_moments.source_phrase: they don't drive cars. They don't have cell phones. Therefore, they must be stupid
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It explicitly reverses the modern prejudice the lecture is trying to undo.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: A story that explains world origins, human identity, social taboos, and the legal order of a society.
One condition of religious authority: the cosmology must feel vast and huge.
One condition of religious authority: everything seen in the world must be explainable within the cosmology.
One condition of religious authority: the system needs a beginning and an end that make it a unified whole.
The invisible, more-real dimension where the essence or soul resides and that the shaman can see or communicate with.
Jiang's counter to technological prejudice: complexity, imagination, dress, ritual, and cosmology reveal sophistication even without modern technology.

uncertainty_notes.note: Student answers to Jiang's questions are mostly absent or fragmentary; claims should rely on Jiang's restatements, not inferred student wording.
Terms such as 'He Spirit,' 'Anakana,' and related proper names are preserved from the transcript and may require checking against the quoted source before canon use.
The student exchange about the shaman image is noisy and partially unintelligible, so the semantic pass only records Jiang's stable conclusion about organization, status, and ritual order.
